_meta.title: Public Key II — RSA Internals & Padding

questions.question: What does the acronym RSA stand for?
questions.explanation: RSA is named after its inventors: Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman, who published the algorithm in 1978.

questions.question: The security of RSA encryption relies on the computational difficulty of what problem?
options: The elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem
questions.explanation: RSA security depends on the difficulty of factoring the modulus n into its prime factors p and q. If an attacker can factor n, they can compute the private key d from the public key (n, e).

questions.question: Why is textbook RSA (without padding) considered insecure?
options: It is too slow for practical use
It only works with even-length messages
It is deterministic, malleable, and provides no semantic security
It requires the sender to know the private key
questions.explanation: Textbook RSA is deterministic (same plaintext → same ciphertext), malleable (ciphertexts can be manipulated to produce predictable plaintext changes), and lacks semantic security (an attacker can test guesses). Proper padding like OAEP is essential.

questions.question: What security property does OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding) provide that PKCS#1 v1.5 padding lacks?
options: Protection against chosen-ciphertext attacks and padding oracle attacks
questions.explanation: OAEP is provably secure against chosen-ciphertext attacks in the random oracle model, whereas PKCS#1 v1.5 is vulnerable to Bleichenbacher's padding oracle attack, which can recover plaintext with about 2^20 chosen-ciphertext queries.

questions.question: What mathematical problem is RSA's security based on?
options: The difficulty of factoring large semiprimes (products of two large primes)
The shortest vector problem in lattices
The difficulty of solving linear equations
questions.explanation: RSA security relies on the fact that multiplying two large primes is easy, but factoring their product back into the primes is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large numbers (2048+ bits). The public key contains the product; the private key requires knowing the factors.
